approach: Use a hash set to track seen numbers. If a number repeats, there is a cycle and it is not happy. If we reach 1, it is happy.
approach_steps: Compute sum of squares of digits of current number
If result is 1, return true
If result is already in set, return false (cycle)
Add result to set and repeat
approach_diagram: n → sumSquares(n) → check set → add to set → repeat

template.python:
class Solution:
    def isHappy(self, n: int) -> bool:

mistakes: Forgetting that happy numbers converge to 1, not loop forever
Using Floyd's cycle detection without handling the case where the cycle doesn't include 1

premium_content.interview_notes: Hash set approach is simpler. Floyd's cycle detection uses O(1) space but harder to implement. Both are acceptable.
edge_cases: Large n causing overflow in digit sum

approach: Use two maps to ensure bijection: s→t and t→s. Every character in s maps to exactly one in t and vice versa.
approach_steps: If lengths differ, return false
Iterate through both strings simultaneously
Check s→t mapping: if exists, must match; else add
Check t→s mapping: if exists, must match; else add
approach_diagram: s[i] ↔ t[i]: mapS2T[s[i]]=t[i] && mapT2S[t[i]]=s[i]

template.python:
class Solution:
    def isIsomorphic(self, s: str, t: str) -> bool:

mistakes: Only checking one direction of mapping (s→t) without checking t→s
Forgetting to handle different length strings

premium_content.interview_notes: Must check both directions. Example: 'ab' and 'aa' fails because 'a' maps to both 'a' and 'b' in t→s direction.

approach: Use a hash set to store elements of one array, then iterate through the other checking for membership. Collect unique results.
approach_steps: Add all elements of nums1 to a set
Iterate through nums2, if element exists in set, add to result set
Convert result set to array
approach_diagram: set1 = set(nums1) → for x in nums2: if x in set1 → add to result

template.python:
class Solution:
    def intersection(self, nums1: List[int], nums2: List[int]) -> List[int]:

mistakes: Not handling duplicates in result (result must be unique)
Confusing intersection with intersection-ii which allows duplicates

premium_content.interview_notes: Two versions exist: one with unique results (this) and one with duplicates. Clarify with interviewer which one.

approach: Count frequencies, then use bucket sort where index represents frequency. Collect from highest frequency bucket down.
approach_steps: Count frequency of each element using a HashMap
Create buckets where index = frequency, value = list of elements
Traverse buckets from highest to lowest frequency, collect k elements
approach_diagram: count → buckets[freq] = [elements] → collect from end → result

template.python:
class Solution:
    def topKFrequent(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> List[int]:

mistakes: Using a max-heap which gives O(n log n) instead of O(n) bucket sort
Forgetting that bucket sort works because frequency range is bounded by n

premium_content.interview_notes: Bucket sort achieves O(n). Heap approach is O(n log k). Quickselect is O(n) average but O(n²) worst. Mention all three.
edge_cases: k equals number of unique elements

approach: Use prefix sum with a hashmap. For each position, check if (current_sum - k) exists in the map. If yes, those are valid subarrays.
approach_steps: Initialize map with {0: 1} (empty prefix)
Compute running prefix sum
Check if (sum - k) exists in map, add count to result
Increment count of current sum in map
approach_diagram: prefixSum → check map[prefixSum - k] → add count → map[prefixSum]++

template.python:
class Solution:
    def subarraySum(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> int:

mistakes: Forgetting to initialize map with {0: 1} for subarrays starting at index 0
Using the wrong order (checking map before updating it vs after)

premium_content.interview_notes: The {0:1} initialization is critical. Without it, subarrays starting from index 0 are missed. This pattern is used in many prefix sum problems.

approach: Add all numbers to a set. Only start counting from a number that is the start of a sequence (num-1 not in set). Count consecutive from there.
approach_steps: Add all numbers to a set
For each num, if num-1 is NOT in set, it is a sequence start
Count consecutive elements from num upward
Track maximum length
approach_diagram: set → for num: if num-1 not in set → count up from num → maxLen

template.python:
class Solution:
    def longestConsecutive(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:

mistakes: Sorting the array first which makes it O(n log n) instead of O(n)
Not checking if num-1 exists, causing redundant work on non-start elements

premium_content.interview_notes: The key insight is only starting from sequence beginnings. This avoids O(n²) nested loops. Sorting approach is acceptable but not optimal.

approach: Use a sliding window of size p.length with frequency maps. Compare frequencies of window and pattern. Slide window by removing leftmost and adding rightmost.
approach_steps: Build frequency map for pattern p
Initialize window frequency map for first |p| characters
Slide window: remove leftmost char, add rightmost char
Compare maps at each step; if equal, add left index to result
approach_diagram: fmap_p → window of |p| → slide: remove s[i], add s[i+|p|] → compare → result

template.python:
class Solution:
    def findAnagrams(self, s: str, p: str) -> List[int]:

mistakes: Using sorting to compare anagrams which is O(k log k) per window
Forgetting to handle the case where s is shorter than p

premium_content.interview_notes: Optimization: use a single counter with matches count instead of comparing entire maps. Decrement when char goes out of range, increment when in range.

approach: Build a graph of character dependencies by comparing adjacent words. First differing character gives an edge (before→after). Then perform topological sort (BFS/Kahn's).
approach_steps: Compare each pair of adjacent words to find first differing character
Build adjacency list and in-degree map
Start BFS with characters having 0 in-degree
Process queue: reduce in-degree of neighbors, add to queue if 0
If result length != unique chars count, cycle exists
approach_diagram: words → build graph (first diff char) → Kahn's BFS → topological order

complexity._note: C = total characters across all words

template.python:
class Solution:
    def alienOrder(self, words: List[str]) -> str:

mistakes: Not handling the case where a longer word comes before a shorter prefix word (invalid)
Forgetting to initialize all characters in the graph even if they have no edges

premium_content.interview_notes: This is a classic topological sort application. Edge case: if word[i] is a prefix of word[i+1], it's invalid (shorter word must come first).
edge_cases: Two words with no common prefix

approach: Use backtracking to try all possible mappings from pattern characters to substrings. Maintain bidirectional mapping to ensure bijection.
approach_steps: Use two maps: pattern→word and word→pattern
Recurse on pattern index and string position
Try all possible substring lengths for current pattern char
If mapping exists, verify match; if not, try new mapping
Backtrack by removing mappings when recursion fails
approach_diagram: backtrack(pi, si) → try substr[si:si+len] → map or verify → recurse → undo

template.python:
class Solution:
    def wordPatternMatch(self, pattern: str, s: str) -> bool:

mistakes: Not maintaining bidirectional mapping (allows non-bijection)
Forgetting to try different substring lengths (only trying one length)

premium_content.interview_notes: This is the backtracking version of word-pattern (which is the easy version with words already split). The bidirectional mapping is key.

approach: Use two maps: key→node and freq→doubly linked list of nodes. Track min frequency. On access, move node to next freq list. On eviction, remove from min freq list.
approach_steps: Maintain keyMap: key → {value, freq, nodeRef}
Maintain freqMap: freq → doubly linked list of nodes
On get: update freq (move node to freq+1 list), return value
On put: if at capacity, remove LFU node; add new node to freq=1 list
Update minFreq as needed
approach_diagram: keyMap + freqMap → get/update freq → put/evict LFU → O(1) all ops
mistakes: Using a simple counter instead of maintaining per-frequency linked lists
Not updating min frequency correctly when all nodes at min freq are evicted
Forgetting to remove empty frequency lists

premium_content.interview_notes: This is one of the hardest design problems. Use OrderedDict in Python or LinkedHashMap in Java for O(1) operations. Key insight: freqMap allows O(1) LFU eviction.

approach: Use a sorted structure (like TreeMap or balanced BST) combined with a hashmap. Alternatively, use bucket-based approach with min/max tracking.
approach_steps: Maintain a TreeMap/SortedMap from value → count
Maintain a HashMap from key → value
insert: add to map, update sorted structure
remove: remove from map, update sorted structure
getMin/getMax: return first/last key from sorted structure
approach_diagram: HashMap<key,val> + TreeMap<val, count> → O(log n) ops → optimize to O(1)
mistakes: Trying to maintain sorted order with arrays which gives O(n) operations
Not handling the case where a key's count reaches 0 (must remove from structure)

premium_content.interview_notes: Similar to LFU cache design. Use TreeMap in Java (O(log n)) or OrderedDict in Python. True O(1) requires bucket-based approach.

approach: Use slow and fast pointers. Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
approach_steps: Initialize slow and fast at head
Move slow one step, fast two steps
When fast is null or fast.next is null, slow is middle
Return slow
approach_diagram: slow += 1, fast += 2 → fast.end → slow = middle
mistakes: Off-by-one error when list has even length (two middles, return second)
Forgetting to handle empty list or single node

premium_content.interview_notes: For even length, this returns the second middle node. If first middle is needed, stop when fast.next.next is null.

approach: Use a slow pointer to track the last unique node. Fast pointer scans ahead. When values differ, link slow to fast's next.
approach_steps: Initialize slow at head
For each fast pointer position, compare values
If different, link slow.next = fast, advance slow
If same, advance fast only
Return head
approach_diagram: slow.unique → fast scans → diff found → slow.next = fast → slow++
mistakes: Not handling empty list
Losing reference to head when skipping duplicates at the start

premium_content.interview_notes: This is for remove-duplicates (keep one copy). For remove-duplicates-ii (remove all duplicates), use a dummy node and skip entire groups.

approach: Traverse both lists simultaneously, adding corresponding digits plus carry. Create result list node by node. Handle remaining carry at the end.
approach_steps: Initialize dummy node and carry = 0
While l1 or l2 or carry > 0: sum = val1 + val2 + carry
Create new node with sum % 10
Update carry = sum / 10
Advance pointers and repeat
approach_diagram: l1 + l2 → sum = l1.val + l2.val + carry → node(sum%10) → carry = sum/10
mistakes: Forgetting to handle carry after both lists are exhausted
Not handling lists of different lengths (one may be null)

premium_content.interview_notes: Numbers are stored in reverse order (least significant digit first). If forward order, reverse first or use recursion/stack.

approach: Split list in half using fast/slow pointers. Reverse second half. Merge two halves alternately.
approach_steps: Find middle using fast/slow pointers
Split into two halves (cut after slow)
Reverse the second half
Merge two halves: take one node from each alternately
approach_diagram: list → split → reverse second half → merge(head, reversed)

#         self.next = next
class Solution:
    def reorderList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> None:
        """
        Do not return anything, modify head in-place instead.
        """

mistakes: Forgetting to cut the list after middle (causes infinite loop)
Not handling odd-length list correctly during merge

premium_content.interview_notes: Three distinct steps: find middle, reverse second half, merge. Each step is a standard linked list operation. Modify in-place.

approach: Use two pointers with n gap. Advance first pointer n steps, then move both together. When first reaches end, second is just before the node to remove.
approach_steps: Create dummy node before head
Advance first pointer n+1 steps
Move both pointers until first is null
Second pointer is now at node before target
Remove target: second.next = second.next.next
approach_diagram: dummy → fast += n → slow and fast together → fast=null → remove slow.next
mistakes: Not using dummy node (removing head requires special handling)
Off-by-one in gap calculation (should be n+1 steps for first)

premium_content.interview_notes: Dummy node simplifies edge case of removing head. First pointer advances n+1 steps so second lands on node BEFORE target.
edge_cases: List with two nodes, remove second

approach: First pass: create all new nodes and map original→copy. Second pass: set next and random pointers using the map.
approach_steps: First pass: for each node, create copy, store in map[original]=copy
Second pass: for each node, set copy.next = map[original.next], copy.random = map[original.random]
Return map[head]
approach_diagram: pass1: create nodes, map[orig]=copy → pass2: set next/random from map

template.python:
# class Node:
#     def __init__(self, x: int, next: 'Node' = None, random: 'Node' = None):
#         self.val = int(x)
#         self.next = next
#         self.random = random
class Solution:
    def copyRandomList(self, head: 'Optional[Node]') -> 'Optional[Node]':

mistakes: Creating copies in the wrong order (random pointers may reference not-yet-created nodes)
Forgetting that random can be null

premium_content.interview_notes: Alternative O(1) space approach: interweave copies (A→A'→B→B'...), set random pointers, then split lists. But HashMap approach is cleaner for interviews.
edge_cases: Random pointer points to earlier node

approach: Check if k nodes exist. If yes, reverse them. Connect reversed group to previous group. Repeat until fewer than k nodes remain.
approach_steps: Count nodes from current position
If fewer than k nodes remain, stop
Reverse k nodes (standard reverse)
Connect: prev.next = newHead of reversed group
Update prev to tail of reversed group
approach_diagram: check k nodes → reverse k → connect to prev group → move prev → repeat
mistakes: Not checking if k nodes exist before reversing
Breaking links during reversal losing reference to next group

premium_content.interview_notes: This is iterative reversal in groups. First count k nodes, then reverse them as a unit. Use dummy node to simplify head handling.

approach: Use merge sort: split list in half recursively, sort each half, merge sorted halves. Use fast/slow pointers to find middle.
approach_steps: Base case: if head is null or single node, return head
Find middle using fast/slow pointers
Recursively sort left and right halves
Merge two sorted halves (standard merge)
approach_diagram: split → sort(left) + sort(right) → merge(left, right) → return
mistakes: Using quicksort (poor performance on linked lists, no random access)
Forgetting to cut the list after finding middle

premium_content.interview_notes: Merge sort is ideal for linked lists: O(n log n) time, O(1) space with careful implementation. Quicksort is poor because of no random access.

approach: Use DFS/recursion. When encountering a child, recursively flatten the child list, then splice it between current node and next node.
approach_steps: Traverse the list using current pointer
If current has a child, save current.next
Recursively flatten child list
Connect: current.next = childHead, childHead.prev = current
Connect: childTail.next = saved next
Continue traversal
approach_diagram: traverse → child? → flatten(child) → splice between current and next

#         self.val = val
#         self.prev = prev
#         self.next = next
#         self.child = child
class Solution:
    def flatten(self, head: 'Optional[Node]') -> 'Optional[Node]':

mistakes: Losing reference to next node before flattening child
Not setting prev pointers correctly in doubly linked list

premium_content.interview_notes: This is DFS on a linked list with child pointers. Iterative approach uses a stack. Both recursive and iterative are acceptable.

approach: Use Floyd's tortoise and hare. Find meeting point inside cycle. Reset one pointer to head, move both at same speed. They meet at cycle start.
approach_steps: Initialize slow and fast at head
Move slow 1 step, fast 2 steps until they meet or fast is null
If no cycle, return null
Reset slow to head, keep fast at meeting point
Move both 1 step at a time; meeting point is cycle start
approach_diagram: slow/fast meet in cycle → reset slow=head → both += 1 → meet at start
mistakes: Forgetting the second phase (resetting to head and moving 1 step)
Not checking if fast or fast.next is null before advancing

premium_content.interview_notes: Mathematical proof: if cycle starts at position a, and meeting point is m steps from start of cycle, then head to cycle start = meeting point to cycle start.
